title: Context-aware operator-gap candidate
notes: Candidate generated after the protocol-bound context test showed that the dynamic/context module should not absorb operator-gap cases. This graph tests operator value, opportunity, recoverability, analogy, repair, and context channels as a scoped operator-gap module. It is not scored pending critique.

conditioning_axes.community: Specify the speech or judgment community before interpreting licensing, production, usage, or repair evidence.
conditioning_axes.norm_centre: Specify whether the relevant norm centre is theoretical, standard-institutional, local-community, or task-specific.
conditioning_axes.genre: Specify the construction's genre or activity context before interpreting acceptability or repair.
conditioning_axes.medium: Specify whether evidence comes from speech, edited prose, elicited written examples, or another medium.
conditioning_axes.task_framing: Specify whether the task asks for acceptability, grammaticality, paraphrase availability, naturalness, or repair.
conditioning_axes.speaker_identity: Specify speaker or writer cues when social indexing could perturb judgment or attribution.

edges.rationale: Observed usage depends on the number of relevant contexts where the operator-gap form or competitor could have appeared.
Opportunity mass calibrates how strongly absence or rarity bears on licensing in the specified community and construction type.
Usage frequency is evidence for licensing only after opportunity and competitor structure are considered.
Analogical support can be evidence that the form belongs near a licensed constructional neighborhood without equaling licensing.
For operator-gap cases only, a missing or mis-set public-update value may be a component of licensing failure; this remains a scoped theoretical wager.
Analogical support can make a form producible or tempting even when licensing remains unsettled.
Community licensing predicts production availability in the specified constructional context.
Licensing trouble predicts pressure toward repair, reformulation, or available paraphrase.
Recoverable meaning can raise reported acceptability without licensing the operator configuration.
Processing burden can lower reported acceptability independently of operator licensing.
Genre conditions whether the form or its repair is appropriate in the task context.
Medium affects how examples, repairs, and judgments are interpreted.
Register or genre mismatch can affect reported acceptability without deciding operator licensing.
Task framing can foreground acceptability, grammaticality, paraphrase availability, naturalness, or repair.
Task framing can change explicit attribution by cueing which evidential channel the participant should treat as relevant.
Speaker or writer cues can change the social interpretation of an example.
Social-indexical associations can perturb reported acceptability without changing the operator-gap analysis.
A specified norm centre can generate explicit condemnation independently of operator licensing.
Correctness ideology can perturb reported acceptability in explicit judgment contexts.
Condemnation can influence attribution while remaining separable from licensing.
Community licensing is evidence for attribution, not identical to attribution.
Repair or paraphrase pressure is evidence about licensing trouble without being the target itself.
Acceptability reports provide noisy evidence for grammaticality attribution.
